outline.emotional_arc: The world ends on a Tuesday evening and she doesn't know how to mourn a place that's still under her feet.
key_events: Mum tells her they're moving to Czech Republic to be near Babička and Děda — they're sitting at the kitchen table, the radiator is off because it's summer, Klára's hands are wrapped around a mug of tea that's gone cold, she says it like she's sorry, like she already knows it's a wound

Anna screams — not crying, screaming, the kind that comes from somewhere below the stomach, the kind that doesn't ask for anything, just exits — and throws her Samsung Galaxy at the wall, the screen cracks in a spiderweb pattern, she doesn't care, she will never care, the phone still worked, she used it cracked for months like a badge

The last week is a blur of endings she didn't get to choose — the last Nando's with Dad where he kept clearing his throat and she kept eating chips like nothing was wrong, the last Saturday at Sainsbury's café where Mum bought her TWO magazines like that would fix it

Last day in London at Elara Chung's flat — they ordered Domino's (pepperoni passion, garlic dip, the Fanta she was never allowed at home), they cried until their faces were hot and swollen, Elara gave her a friendship bracelet that Anna still wears, the pink thread is grey now, they promised to FaceTime every day, they didn't, not her fault, not Elara's, just what happens

Driving away from the flat above Ladbrokes — she didn't look back, she looked at the cracked screen in her lap and thought about the man inside the radiator and whether he'd miss her, which is stupid, he's not real, she knows he's not real, but she thought it anyway
new_or_changed_relationships: Klára Liebigová — the move was Mum's decision and Anna knew it, felt it, held it against her before she even understood what holding meant; Mum became the person who took England away, and the gratitude for Babička's house came later, much later, not yet

Marcus Liebig — he didn't fight the move, or if he did she didn't see it, and his silence about it was its own kind of abandonment; she would remember later that he said 'it might be good for you' and she would never forgive that sentence

Elara Chung — the goodbye was too big for two ten-year-olds and they performed it like they understood what 'never seeing each other at school again' meant, but they didn't, not yet; the FaceTime calls went from daily to weekly to monthly to a happy birthday text, and the friendship bracelet is the only proof it was real
outline.internal_state: Rage. Not sadness — rage. The kind that makes you throw things. The kind that lives in your jaw and your fists and the way you walk. She didn't cry when they drove away because crying would mean it was real and she wasn't ready for real yet. She held the cracked phone like a weapon. She didn't say a single Czech word for the entire drive. She didn't know this was the beginning of choosing silence when the world chose for her.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: The routine was already dying and she could feel it. Wake up — no school, it was summer — to the sound of the flat being dismantled. Mum had started packing boxes in the second week of June, newspaper-wrapped mugs and books going into cardboard from the Tesco on Garratt Lane. Anna lay in bed listening to the tape gun and refusing to help. Breakfast was whatever, toast if she made it herself, sometimes nothing, sometimes Mum left a banana on the counter like that counted. The phone with the cracked screen sat face-up on her pillow, the spiderweb pattern catching the morning light through the gap in the curtains. She checked it first thing — messages from Elara Chung, mostly memes, sometimes just 'this sucks' and Anna would reply 'I know' and that was enough. The days had no structure. Sometimes she walked to the park near Fountain Road alone, sat on the swing, scrolled through photos she'd already seen. Sometimes she went to Elara's flat and they didn't do anything, just existed in the same room watching YouTube compilations on Elara's laptop — try not to laugh challenges, Minecraft speedruns, the usual. 'Old Town Road' was everywhere that summer, every shop, every phone speaker, and she'd hum it without meaning to and then hate herself for humming. The flat got emptier. The bookshelf went. The mugs went. The family photos came off the wall and Mum put them in a shoebox and Anna watched and didn't help. The radiator was off because it was June but she still flinched when the pipe knocked at night, out of habit, out of something older than habit. Dinner was whatever Mum cooked from the remaining pots, nothing tasted right, everything tasted like leaving. She went to bed late, later than Mum wanted, lying in the half-empty room with the cracked phone glowing, reading old texts from Elara, from Dad, from nobody, just scrolling, just not sleeping. The days were long and short at the same time. The weeks were counting down. She could feel the number getting smaller and she hated it.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the kitchen table
key_scenes.what_happened: It was a Tuesday, she thinks it was a Tuesday, early June, still light outside at 8pm because English summer does that. Mum sat her down at the kitchen table and the radiator was off, no knocking, no fist, just silence, and that was worse somehow. Klára had a mug of tea she wasn't drinking. Her hands were wrapped around it like she was cold but it wasn't cold, it was June, she wasn't cold. She said they were moving. She said 'Babička and Děda need us' and 'it'll be good for your Czech' and 'I've already talked to Dad about it' and each sentence was a brick in a wall Anna couldn't see over. Anna didn't cry. She screamed — not a scream that asked for anything, not a scream that wanted comfort, just the sound that came out when her chest couldn't hold what was inside it. The Samsung Galaxy was in her hand and she threw it at the wall above the counter and the crack spread like a web, like roots, like the thing she couldn't take back. The phone hit the lino. The screen was shattered. Klára didn't move. Klára just sat there with her cold tea and her sorry face and Anna hated her, really hated her, for the first time, and the hate was clean and sharp and easier than anything else she could feel.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Rage. Pure rage. The kind that has nowhere to go so it goes everywhere. The kind that makes you throw the thing in your hand before you decide to throw it. Under the rage: a terror she couldn't name, the feeling of the ground being pulled out from under her, except the ground was still there, she was still sitting on the chair, the flat was still the flat, nothing had changed yet and everything had changed already.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The skin forming on top of Mum's cold tea — that thin film on the surface, the way it wrinkled when Klára's hands shifted the mug, and Anna stared at it and thought 'that's what I look like on the inside, a skin forming over something I'm not allowed to touch.'

key_scenes.location: Nando's on Garratt Lane, the booth by the window
key_scenes.what_happened: The last Sunday. He ordered too much — half chicken, extra chips, garlic bread, the lot — and she ate the chips one by one, slowly, making them last, because when the chips were gone the Nando's was gone and when the Nando's was gone England was gone. He kept clearing his throat. He said 'it might be good for you, Munch' and she heard every word and filed them away to hate later. He said 'you'll pick up Czech proper, won't you?' like it was a holiday, like she was going on a trip, like the flat above Ladbrokes would still be there when she got back. He didn't cry. He kept clearing his throat. He didn't say 'I'll miss you' — he said 'we'll do video calls, yeah?' and the 'yeah' went up at the end like a Czech question and she wanted to scream again but she'd already thrown one phone and she didn't have another one to throw. The bill came. He paid. They walked to the car park and he hugged her too tight and she let him and she didn't hug back, just stood there with her arms at her sides, and the not-hugging-back was its own kind of scream.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: A specific fury at his calm, at his clearing throat, at the way he talked about Czech like it was an opportunity and not a sentence. Under the fury: the knowledge that he wasn't fighting it, that he'd agreed to this, that he was letting her go without a war, and the not-fighting was worse than anything he could have said. She wanted him to shout. She wanted him to say 'she's not going.' He didn't. He cleared his throat and ordered garlic bread and she ate her chips and the silence between them was the loudest thing in the restaurant.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The Peri-Peri salt on her fingers from the chips, the way it stayed even after she wiped them on the napkin, the taste of it when she bit her thumbnail later in the car — Nando's salt, the last English salt she'd taste for months, and she didn't know that yet but her body knew.

key_scenes.location: Elara Chung's flat, Tooting Broadway, the sitting room
key_scenes.what_happened: The last day. The absolute last day. They ordered Domino's — pepperoni passion, garlic dip, the Fanta she was never allowed at home — and they watched YouTube and they didn't talk about it and then they did talk about it and then they couldn't stop talking about it. 'I'll FaceTime you every day,' Elara said, and Anna said 'every day,' and they both meant it, they were ten, they didn't know what 'every day' cost. Elara gave her a friendship bracelet — pink thread, woven, the kind you make at lunch with embroidery floss — and Anna put it on and didn't take it off, not that night, not in the car, not ever. They cried. Not cute crying. The ugly kind where your face gets hot and your nose runs and you can't breathe right. Elara's mum came in at some point and said 'girls, the pizza's getting cold' and they laughed through the crying which made it worse, somehow, the laughing-through-crying is always worse. When it was time to go they hugged in the hallway and the hug lasted too long and also not long enough and Anna walked down the stairs of Elara's building and the concrete was cold through her trainers and she thought 'this is the last time I'll walk down these stairs' and she was right.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Grief that was too big for her body. The grief of losing the one person who didn't need her to translate herself. The grief of losing the backwards language and the Wii and the noodles and the flat that didn't have crying in the bathroom. Under the grief: the fierce determination to not let it go, to FaceTime every day, to keep it alive, and the part of her that already knew — even at ten, even before it happened — that promises made while crying don't hold the way you want them to.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The friendship bracelet going onto her wrist, the pink thread against her skin, Elara's fingers tying it, the little pull when the knot tightened — that specific sensation of being given something you can't put down, being claimed by someone who was claiming you back, and the thread felt alive, like it was holding something, and it was, it was holding the whole friendship.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: The move was Mum's decision and Anna knew it in her bones before Klára confirmed it. Sitting at that kitchen table with the cold tea and the sorry face — Mum wasn't asking, she was telling, and the telling was wrapped in apology but the apology didn't change the telling. Anna started holding the move against her mother like a stone in her pocket, something to touch when she needed to remember who to blame. Every box Mum packed was evidence. Every 'it'll be good for you' was a wound. The gratitude for Babička's house would come later, much later, not yet — now there was only the woman who took England away, and Anna looked at her and saw the enemy, and the enemy was wearing her mother's face.

He didn't fight it. Or if he did, she didn't see it, and the not-seeing was its own kind of evidence. 'It might be good for you, Munch' — six words that would live in her chest like shrappel. He said it while clearing his throat and ordering garlic bread and she would replay it for years, the casualness of it, the way he talked about the end of her world like it was a character-building exercise. His silence about the move was a second abandonment. The first was the spare room. This was the spare room but bigger — the spare country, the spare life, the spare father who let her go without a war. She didn't know yet that his silence might have been grief. She didn't know that men sometimes clear their throats because crying isn't allowed. She only knew he let her leave, and she would never fully forgive that sentence, that 'it might be good for you,' never.

The goodbye was too big for two ten-year-olds and they performed it like they understood what 'never seeing each other at school again' meant, but they didn't, not yet. The pizza, the crying, the bracelet — it was all correct, all the right beats, but underneath it was the panic of two kids who had never lost anyone trying to figure out the choreography of loss. The FaceTime calls went from daily to weekly to monthly to a happy birthday text, and the friendship bracelet is still on her wrist, grey now, and the friendship isn't dead but it's not alive either, it's somewhere in between, in the space where daily used to be. The friendship didn't end. It just thinned. Like the thread on the bracelet, like the signal, like all the things you promise to keep when you're crying and the promise feels like the only thing keeping the world together.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to hold a grudge like a stone in your pocket
knowledge_acquired.how: Mum told her they were moving and Anna reached for the rage because the rage had edges and the sadness didn't. The rage was something she could hold. The rage had a target — Mum, Dad, the flat, the boxes, the whole stupid situation. She learned that anger is easier than grief, cleaner, more directional. You can point anger at someone. Grief just sits on you like weather. She didn't know this was a lesson she was learning. She thought she was just furious. But the pattern was starting — the choosing of rage over sadness, the wearing of fury like armor, and the armor would serve her for years and also trap her for years, but that was a problem for later, for much later, not now, now there was only the stone in the pocket and the hand that wouldn't let go.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to crack a screen and wear it like evidence
knowledge_acquired.how: The Samsung Galaxy hit the kitchen wall and the web-pattern spread across the glass and she didn't care. She used the cracked phone for months after, reading through the spiderweb, and the cracked-ness became the point — look what I did, look what this made me do, look at the evidence of how much this hurt. She learned that sometimes breaking something is the only way to show what's happening inside you, and sometimes the broken thing is more honest than the whole thing ever was. The crack was a story she could tell without having to explain. 'What happened to your phone?' 'I threw it.' The end. No follow-up questions. No feelings required.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Goodbyes are rehearsals for something you can't rehearse
knowledge_acquired.how: The last Nando's, the last Saturday café, the last day at Elara's — she did all of them correctly, said the right things, hugged the right people, ate the last chips and tied the bracelet and performed the farewell. None of it prepared her for the car, for the motorway, for the way England kept being England outside the window as they drove toward Dover, like nothing had changed, like the world didn't know she was leaving, like the world didn't care. She learned that goodbyes don't work. They're a ritual you complete and then the real thing happens later, alone, in a room that doesn't smell right, and no one is watching, and you finally let yourself feel it, and it's too late to say any of the things you rehearsed.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum took England away
opinion_snapshot.because: The kitchen table, the cold tea, the sorry face that wasn't sorry enough to change the plan. Mum decided. Mum packed. Mum drove. Anna didn't get a vote. She didn't get a say. She got boxes and a flat in Benešov and 'it'll be good for your Czech' and the Czech was never the point, the point was being asked, the point was being considered, the point was someone giving a single shit about what she wanted.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Dad didn't fight for her
opinion_snapshot.because: He said 'it might be good for you.' He cleared his throat. He ordered garlic bread. He didn't say 'she's not going.' He didn't say 'over my dead body.' He said 'we'll do video calls' and the 'calls' was already a concession, already an acceptance, already the white flag. She would never forgive that sentence. She would replay it for years, looking for the love in it, the fight in it, and finding only the clearing throat and the garlic bread and the bill he paid without arguing.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The cracked phone is a badge, not a wound
opinion_snapshot.because: She threw it. She broke it. She did that. Not the move, not Mum, not Czech — her hand, her arm, her rage made physical. The web-pattern on the screen was proof that she had felt something so big it had to break something, and she wore it cracked for months like evidence. When people asked 'what happened to your phone?' she said 'I threw it' and watched their faces and the watching was the point. The crack was a story. The crack was a way of saying 'I'm not okay' without having to say it.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Goodbyes don't work
opinion_snapshot.because: She said goodbye to Elara and it didn't stick. She said goodbye to the flat and it didn't feel like enough. She said goodbye to Nando's and the chips tasted the same as always and the sameness was an insult. Goodbyes are supposed to make the leaving feel like something but they don't, they just make you perform feelings you're not ready to have, and then the feelings come later, in the wrong place, at the wrong time, when nobody's watching and there's no one to say them to.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: The cracked-screen vocabulary — 'whatever,' 'fine,' 'I don't care' — short words that close doors
slang_and_language.origin: The throw. After the phone hit the wall she started using language like she used the crack — as evidence, as armor. Short sentences when she was uncomfortable, which was always. 'Whatever' as a shield. 'Fine' as a lie. 'I don't care' as the biggest lie. She learned that you can say less and feel more, that silence can be a weapon, that English is good for walls because it doesn't invite follow-up questions the way Czech does.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Refusing Czech — not speaking a single Czech word during the drive, during the ferry, during the first days, like English was the last piece of England she could keep
slang_and_language.origin: The move. The kitchen table. The knowledge that Czech was the language of the place she was going and English was the language of the place she was leaving, and every Czech word she said was a step away from the flat above Ladbrokes and toward whatever came next. She held English like a rope. She didn't speak Czech for the entire drive, the entire ferry, the first week in Benešov, and the silence was deliberate, was political, was the only vote she had.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — but weaponised now, said at Mum, not with Mum
slang_and_language.origin: The fridge bump was accidental. The new 'proboha' was aimed. When Mum said 'it'll be okay, Anna,' Anna said 'proboha' like Mum said it — sharp, tired, for God's sake — and she watched Klára flinch and the flinching was the point. She took Mum's word and used it against her. The word had always been about the fridge and the bumping and the not-learning, but now it was about the move, the boxes, the cold tea, and every 'proboha' was a small revenge that didn't make her feel better but she did it anyway.

cringe_moments.moment: At the last Nando's, Dad cleared his throat and said 'we'll do video calls, yeah?' and Anna said 'I don't want your video calls' and then immediately wished she could take it back but couldn't, the words were already in the air, and Dad's face did something she'd never seen before — not hurt exactly, but empty, like someone had unplugged him — and she spent the rest of the meal trying to figure out how to unsay a thing you can't unsay, and she never figured it out, and the last words she said to him in that Nando's were mean and the mean wasn't even true, she did want his calls, she wanted them more than anything, she just couldn't say the true thing when the false thing came out first.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because it wasn't true. Because she wanted the calls. Because his face went empty and she did that. Because the last Nando's was supposed to be about chips and clearing throats and instead it was about cruelty she didn't mean and couldn't take back. Because she still thinks about his face when she can't sleep. Because 'I don't want your video calls' lives in her head rent-free and the eviction notice never comes.

cringe_moments.moment: She told Elara Chung she'd FaceTime 'every single day, I promise, every day' and she meant it with her whole body, she was crying when she said it, the bracelet was still warm from Elara's fingers, and she believed it, she genuinely believed it, and then she didn't do it, not because she didn't want to but because the days got full and the time difference was only an hour but felt like more and the calls got shorter and the silences got longer, and now it's a promise she broke and you can't glue a promise back together like a phone screen, it just stays broken, and she still wears the bracelet and the bracelet knows she's a liar.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because Elara believed her. Because the promise was real when she made it. Because breaking a promise isn't the same as lying but it feels the same. Because the bracelet is grey now and the pink it used to be is the color of a promise she couldn't keep and the grey is the color of what it became.

cringe_moments.moment: In the car, pulling away from the flat above Ladbrokes for the last time, Mum asked 'are you okay, Anna?' and Anna said 'I'm fine' in the flattest voice she'd ever used and then she thought about the man inside the radiator and whether he'd miss her, and the thought was so stupid and so childlike and so embarrassing that she wanted to throw the phone again, except the phone was already broken, and you can't break something that's already broken, and the thought about the radiator man sat inside her like a secret, the kind of secret you'd die before sharing, because you're ten and you're supposed to be too old for imaginary men in pipes but apparently you're not, apparently you're exactly the right age for one last stupid thought about a stupid flat you're never going back to.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she still thinks about him sometimes. The radiator man. The not-real person she invented when she was too young to understand central heating. She's fourteen now and she knows about pipes and boilers and expansion noises but part of her — the part that doesn't listen to reason, the part that still flinches at knocking — still wonders if he noticed she left. The embarrassment isn't about believing in him. It's about still, somewhere, believing in him.

cringe_moments.moment: At the Sainsbury's café on the last Saturday, Mum bought her TWO magazines — the Beano and a Minecraft one — and said 'see? It's not all bad' and Anna looked at her and felt the rage rise and instead of screaming she just said 'thanks' in a voice that made 'thanks' sound like a slur, and the woman at the next table glanced over, and Mum's face did the tight thing, and Anna sat there with her two magazines like a spoiled brat who couldn't accept a gift and she knew she was being awful and she couldn't stop, the awful was the only thing that felt true.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because Mum was trying. Because two magazines was Mum's language, the language of small offerings, and Anna spat on it. Because she can still see the woman at the next table looking at her like she was the problem. Because maybe she was the problem. Because 'thanks' sounded like a knife and she'd meant it to and the meaning it was the part she couldn't take back.

outline.emotional_arc: Silence is a language and she speaks it fluently — three months of drowning in a country that sounds like static.
key_events: Arrived at Babička's house on Na Purkrabství — yellow exterior, cherry trees in the garden, the smell of lavender and old wood and something baking, the attic room with slanted ceilings that became hers, she put up the London poster she brought and the bed creaked and you could hear church bells at night and it was too quiet, so quiet she could hear her own heartbeat, she hated the quiet

Started at ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova — grey building, Czech flag in every classroom, smelled like boiled cabbage at lunch, she sat at the back and understood nothing, not a word, the sounds were the sounds from the flat but wrong, faster, directed at her, no translation, no Dad to make them friendly

Tereza Bártová whispered 'angličanka' on the second day and some kids laughed — not cruel, not really, just the way kids mark what's different, but Anna felt the word land like a stone, she didn't know the word was coming but she felt it arrive

The toilet incident — she tried to say 'potřebuju na záchod' (I need the toilet) but used the wrong word, said something like 'potřebuju díru' (I need the hole), the whole class lost it, Mrs. Navrátilová tried to keep a straight face and couldn't, Anna wanted to dissolve into the floor, she didn't speak again for three days after that, not a word, she went to the toilet by pointing

Three months of silence — not elective mutism exactly, just survival, just the decision that it was safer to be quiet than to be wrong, she ate lunch alone or near the football pitch where no one talked to her, she watched the other kids like they were a TV show she couldn't understand, she started counting the hours until she could go home to Babička's kitchen where no one expected her to speak

FaceTime with Dad — he always seemed to be somewhere noisy, a pub, a street, Siân's flat probably, he said 'how's it going, Munch?' and she said 'fine' and the calls got shorter, 15 minutes, then 10, then she started dodging them, then felt guilty, then dodged them again
new_or_changed_relationships: Marie Novotná (Babička) — became the safest person without trying, she made koláče and never pushed, let Anna sit in the kitchen in silence, didn't ask how school was, just put food in front of her and hummed Czech songs and for the first time the Czech sounds weren't anxious, they were warm, Anna didn't know yet that this was the relationship that would save her

Tereza Bártová — enemy by proxy, the girl who named her 'angličanka,' the face that went with the laugh, Anna hated her with the clean hatred of someone who's been humiliated, she didn't know this would become the closest friendship she'd ever have

Marcus Liebig — the FaceTime calls became a chore, not because she didn't love him but because loving him hurt, because his voice was still the low rumble but now it came through a cracked screen and there was always noise in the background and she could hear England without being in it and the distance was a physical ache she couldn't name
outline.internal_state: Drowning. Actually drowning. The feeling of being underwater — sounds muffled, movements slow, everything requiring enormous effort. She didn't know the Czech word for 'drowning' but she was doing it. The silence wasn't peaceful, it was suffocation. She went to bed at 8pm because being awake was exhausting. She dreamed in English and woke up in Czech. The church bells at night sounded like the radiator's knocking and she hated them for it.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: The alarm on Babička's phone went at 6:45 because Babička liked to have the kitchen warm before Anna came down. Anna heard it through the floor — the creak of the old woman's feet on the wooden boards, the click of the gas stove, the rattle of the kettle. She stayed in the attic room until the smell of something reached her, usually rohlíky from the bakery Babička walked to at dawn, sometimes scrambled eggs with the soft bread torn into it, the Czech way, the way Dad never made it. The attic room was cold in September, cold in October, freezing by November, and she'd lie in bed counting the ceiling beams — fourteen — until the smell pulled her down the narrow stairs. Babička never asked if she slept well. Just put food in front of her. Hummed. The walk to ZŠ Benešov took eleven minutes if she walked slow, nine if she walked fast, and she always walked slow because arriving early meant sitting in the corridor alone, which was better than sitting in the classroom with kids who spoke a language she should know but didn't. School started at 8:00. She sat at the back in every class, near the radiator, which knocked, and she hated it for knocking the same way here as it did in London, like the angry man had followed her. Lunch was at 12:15 in the canteen that smelled like boiled cabbage and overcooked carrots, and she ate near the football pitch when weather allowed because no one talked near the football pitch, just kicked balls and screamed and left her alone. Home at 2:30 or 3:00 depending on the day. Babička's kitchen. Homework she couldn't do, staring at Czech worksheets until the letters stopped being letters. The TV in Babička's sitting room, Czech channels, Czech soap operas she half-watched without understanding. Bed at 8:00 because being awake was exhausting and the dark was quieter than the light and the church bells rang at 8:00 too and she hated them but they also meant the day was over and that was something. Three months of this. Same shit different day but in a language she couldn't read.

key_scenes.location: Babička's house on Na Purkrabství, the attic room
key_scenes.what_happened: The first night. She'd brought one thing — a London Underground poster from WHSmith at Clapham Junction, the classic one, red and blue and white, and she Blu-Tacked it to the slanted wall above the bed and the bed creaked when she lay down and the ceiling was so close she could almost touch it and the room smelled like lavender and old wood and something else, something like Babička's perfume but older, and the window faced the church and the church bells hadn't rung yet and the silence was so loud she could hear her own heartbeat, actually hear it, thudding in her ears like the radiator's knocking but internal, and she pulled the duvet up and stared at the poster and the poster was London and she was not in London and the distance between her and the poster was the distance between her and everything she knew. She didn't cry. She was too tired to cry. The church bells rang at 10 and she flinched like she flinched at the radiator and she thought 'the man inside followed me' and she pulled the pillow over her head and breathed into the lavender smell and didn't sleep for hours.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Not sadness. Not yet. Just the wrongness of everything — the ceiling too low, the sounds too different, the air too clean, the quiet too loud. The feeling of being placed somewhere she didn't fit, like a puzzle piece from the wrong box.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The creak of the bed springs when she turned — an old sound, a Babička sound, a sound that had held other bodies for decades before hers, and the slight give of the mattress like the house was breathing underneath her.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, Mrs. Navrátilová's classroom, the back row near the radiator
who_was_there: approximately 24 other students whose names she didn't know
key_scenes.what_happened: The toilet incident, second week of October, maybe Wednesday. She'd been holding it for two hours because asking meant speaking and speaking meant being wrong. But her body wasn't cooperating and the pressure was too much so she raised her hand and Mrs. Navrátilová — patient, kind, the one who didn't treat her like a problem — nodded at her and she stood up and opened her mouth and tried to say 'potřebuju na záchod' which Mum had taught her, drilled into her, but the words came out wrong, they came out 'potřebuju díru' and she knew they were wrong the instant they left her mouth because the room changed, the air changed, Tereza Bártová in the second row turned around and her eyes went wide and then her mouth went wide and then the sound came — not just Tereza, half the class, the word 'díru' hanging in the air like a stink bomb, 'I need a hole,' and Mrs. Navrátilová tried, she actually tried, her lips pressed together and her eyes went shiny with the effort of not laughing and then she couldn't hold it and a small sound escaped, a 'pfft' through her nose, and that made it worse, that made it so much worse, and Anna walked out of the classroom and didn't come back for ten minutes and when she came back no one looked at her and she didn't speak again for three days.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The floor should have opened up and swallowed her. The heat in her face like she was on fire. The wrong word sitting in the room like a living thing, a creature she'd birthed without meaning to, and it was eating her alive. Shame like she'd never felt — not the guilt of listening to Mum cry, not the sadness of Dad's closed door, but the hot sharp shame of being laughable, of being the joke, of having a mouth that betrayed her.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The sound Tereza Bártová made — not a laugh, not yet, a sharp intake of breath first, like the moment before a car crash when you see it coming and can't stop it, and then the laugh, high and bright and cutting.

key_scenes.location: Babička's kitchen on Na Purkrabství, the wooden table by the window
key_scenes.what_happened: The three days of silence after the toilet incident. Anna came home from school and sat at the kitchen table and didn't speak and Babička didn't ask. This was the thing about Babička — she didn't fill silence with questions the way Mum did, didn't push, didn't say 'what's wrong, what happened, tell me.' She just put a plate of koláče on the table — the ones with poppy seed filling, the ones Anna ate without knowing what was in them — and she hummed. The humming was Czech folk songs, old ones, the kind Děda František used to play on the radio before he got sick, and Anna didn't know the words but she knew the tune, her body knew the tune, and she sat in the warm kitchen with the smell of baking and the old woman's voice going low and soft and the Czech sounds weren't anxious here, weren't fast, weren't directed at her like a test she was failing. They were just sounds. Warm sounds. The first warm Czech sounds since the move. She ate four koláče and didn't say thank you because she wasn't speaking and Babička didn't seem to need her to.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: For the first time since arriving, the Czech language didn't feel like water filling her lungs. It felt like a blanket. Not a too-tight Mum-blanket, not an anxious blanket. Just warmth. The radical feeling of being near someone who didn't need anything from her, not words, not responses, not performance. Just presence.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The poppy seeds between her teeth, the slight grit of them, the sweet dough going soft in her mouth, and Babička's humming — low, tune-wandering, the same phrase repeated three times before moving to the next, like she'd forgotten how the song went and was making it up as she went along.

key_scenes.location: The attic room, the bed with the London poster above it
key_scenes.what_happened: A Sunday FaceTime call, late October, the light going grey outside the church. Dad's face in the cracked screen — she could see he was somewhere noisy, a pub maybe, there were voices behind him, glasses clinking, and he said 'alright, Munch? How's it going?' and she said 'fine' and he said 'good, good' and there was a pause where neither of them spoke and she could hear someone laughing in the background, a woman, probably Siân, and Dad looked away from the camera for a second, distracted, and she knew she didn't have all of him, she had a fraction of him, a slice of his attention, and she said 'Dad' and he said 'yeah?' and she had nothing to follow it with, she just wanted to say his name, to make him look at the screen, at her, and he did, for a second, and his eyes were the same, brown and warm, but the warmth was smaller through a screen, like looking at a fire through a window, and the call lasted eleven minutes, she counted, and when it ended she stared at the London poster until the shapes stopped meaning anything and the church bells rang and she pulled the duvet over her head.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The ache was physical. Not stomach-ache, not headache, but somewhere in her chest, a hollow that got bigger when she heard his voice and smaller when the call ended and the screen went dark. She didn't have the word 'homesick' but she had the feeling — sick for home, sick with wanting, sick with the knowledge that home wasn't a place anymore, it was a person she could only see through glass.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The crack in the screen running through Dad's left eye, the way it made him look like he was crying, the way he wasn't crying, the way she was.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: Became the safest person without trying, without deciding, without any conscious effort at all. The kitchen did it. The koláče. The humming. The way she put food in front of Anna and didn't ask how school was, didn't check her face for sadness, didn't say 'proboha' when she bumped into things. Babička's love was the opposite of Mum's — it was light, it was undemanding, it was present without pressure. Anna didn't trust it at first because she'd been trained to expect anxiety from the Czech women in her life, but by November she was seeking it, drifting into the kitchen after school, sitting at the table without speaking, eating whatever appeared, letting the warmth of it fill the hollow that school carved out. She didn't know yet that this relationship would save her. She just knew that Babička's kitchen was the only place in Benešov where she could breathe.

Became the enemy by accident, by one word, by the casual cruelty of children who don't know they're being cruel. 'Angličanka' — the English girl — whispered on the second day, loud enough for the others to hear, and the laugh that followed, and the face attached to the laugh, brown hair, sharp chin, eyes that found Anna's and held them for a beat too long. The toilet incident cemented it. The sound Tereza made — that intake of breath, that bright sharp laugh — it lived in Anna's chest like a splinter. She hated Tereza with a clean hatred, uncomplicated, the hatred of someone who has been marked as different and then been made to feel it. She didn't know this would become the closest friendship she'd ever have. She couldn't know. She only knew that Tereza Bártová was the face that laughed when she was drowning.

The FaceTime calls became a chore, not because she didn't love him but because loving him through a screen was like eating with your hands tied — you could do it but everything was harder and messier and less satisfying. His voice was still the low rumble but it came with background noise now, pub sounds, Siân sounds, England sounds, and she could hear her old country without being in it and the distance was a physical ache she couldn't name. 'How's it going, Munch?' — 'Fine.' The same exchange every week, shrinking, hollowing out, becoming a performance of connection instead of connection itself. She started dodging the calls, then felt guilty, then dodged them again. The guilt was worse than the dodge because she knew he was trying, she could see him trying, but trying wasn't the same as being there and being there was all she wanted and he wasn't there, he was in a flat in Croydon with a woman she'd never met and a life she wasn't part of, and every FaceTime call reminded her of this, every background laugh was proof that England had continued without her.

knowledge_acquired.how: The toilet incident taught her that speaking was dangerous, that her mouth could betray her, that the wrong word could turn an entire room against her. The three days of not speaking weren't a choice exactly — more like a reflex, like pulling your hand away from a hot stove. She learned that if she didn't speak, she couldn't be wrong. If she couldn't be wrong, she couldn't be laughed at. The logic was airtight and the cost was invisibility, but invisibility felt safer than exposure, so she chose it, or it chose her, and by December she was so good at being quiet that it had become a second skin.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to read a room without understanding the words
knowledge_acquired.how: Three months of sitting in classrooms understanding nothing taught her to read bodies instead of language. The way Mrs. Navrátilová's shoulders relaxed when the class was calm. The way the kids in the front row leaned forward when they were interested. The way tension lived in the air before someone got told off. She couldn't follow the Czech but she could follow the energy, and this was a skill she didn't know she was learning, a skill that would make her sharp later, perceptive, the kind of person who knew something was wrong before anyone said it. It started here, in the not-understanding, in the paying-attention-to-everything-else.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Czech folk songs she can hum but not name
knowledge_acquired.how: Babička's kitchen. The humming. Kde domov můj, the national anthem, which Babička sang under her breath on national holidays. Andělíček můj strážníček, the lullaby. Other tunes without names that lived in Babička's throat and moved into Anna's by proximity, by repetition, by the same way she'd learned the radiator's knocking — not consciously, but bodily, so that years later she would hear these songs and her chest would tighten and she wouldn't know why.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Speaking is dangerous
opinion_snapshot.because: The toilet incident. The laugh. The sound her mouth made when it produced the wrong word. Every time she opened her mouth something terrible came out, so it was better to keep it closed. The logic of a drowning person — don't breathe in, don't breathe at all, the water will get in.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Czech is the language that hurts
opinion_snapshot.because: English made Dad laugh. English made biscuits happen. Czech made Mum's face go tight. Czech made the kids in class laugh at her. Czech was the word that meant 'wrong' and 'different' and 'angličanka.' She didn't choose to hate Czech. It happened to her the way the cold happened to her — you don't choose to hate the thing that's freezing you.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Babička's kitchen is the only safe place in this country
opinion_snapshot.because: No one laughed at her there. No one expected her to speak. The humming was the opposite of the radiator's knocking — warm sound instead of angry sound, gentle instead of violent. The koláče appeared without asking. The wood smelled like old and safe and the same. The whole country was a war zone and the kitchen was the bunker.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Dad is becoming a person on a screen, not a person in the world
opinion_snapshot.because: The calls got shorter. The background got noisier. His eyes looked away more. The 'alright, Munch?' became a script, not a question. She could feel him becoming smaller, like a photo left in the sun, fading at the edges, and the fear was that one day she'd look at the screen and there'd be nothing there at all, just the crack running through where his left eye used to be.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Potřebuju na záchod' — said carefully, practiced in her head for hours before saying, and still got it wrong
slang_and_language.origin: Klára drilled this phrase before school started, sitting at the kitchen table in London, making Anna repeat it, 'potřebuju na záchod, potřebuju na záchod,' and Anna said it right thirty times at home and wrong once at school and the once was the one that lived forever.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Pointing instead of speaking — at the toilet, at food in the canteen, at the page when called on in class
slang_and_language.origin: Self-invented survival strategy. The toilet incident proved that Czech was a minefield and her mouth was a liability. Pointing was universal. Pointing couldn't be wrong. Pointing couldn't be laughed at. She became fluent in gesture.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Fine' — the only word she said on FaceTime with Dad, said flat, said small, said like a door closing
slang_and_language.origin: Not from anywhere. Just the only safe answer. Fine wasn't good, which was a lie, and fine wasn't bad, which would make him worry, and fine wasn't true, which was the point — the truth was too big for a cracked screen, too complicated for eleven minutes, too heavy for a Sunday evening.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: The word 'angličanka' — said by Tereza, repeated by others, absorbed like a bruise
slang_and_language.origin: Tereza Bártová, second day of school, whispered but not really, the way kids whisper when they want to be heard. The English girl. The foreign girl. The wrong girl. Anna didn't know the word was coming but she felt it arrive, the way you feel a stone thrown at your chest — the impact before the understanding.

cringe_moments.moment: The toilet incident — 'potřebuju díru' instead of 'potřebuju na záchod,' I need a hole, the whole class laughing, Mrs. Navrátilová trying not to laugh and failing, the sound Tereza made, walking out of the room, walking back in, not speaking for three days.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because it's the moment she decided to stop talking. Because it proved that her mouth was dangerous. Because she can still hear the exact pitch of Tereza's laugh, still feel the heat in her face, still taste the wrong word like something rotten. Because she thinks about it at 3am sometimes and makes a small sound of disgust into the dark and the church bells don't ring at 3am so the sound just sits there, in the room, in her chest, undigested.

cringe_moments.moment: The first time Tereza Bártová said 'angličanka' and Anna didn't know what it meant but she knew it meant her, she knew it was a label, she knew it was the sound of being different, and she just sat there, taking it, not saying anything back.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she should have said something. She doesn't know what, but something. Because the silence felt like agreement, like she was accepting the name, like she was letting it stick. Because 'angličanka' became true — she is the English girl, she is different, she is the one who doesn't belong — and she hates that Tereza saw it before she did.

cringe_moments.moment: Sitting alone at lunch near the football pitch in November, cold, eating a rohlík from Babička's kitchen because she couldn't face the canteen, and a boy — she never knew his name — walked past and said something in Czech and she didn't understand but she understood the tone, the tone was pity, and pity was worse than cruelty.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she preferred being laughed at to being pitied. Because the boy's face was kind and kindness from strangers was unbearable. Because she ate the rest of the rohlík with her back to him so he couldn't see her face doing whatever it was doing.

outline.emotional_arc: The first word that comes out right is a door opening — small, terrifying, and she walks through it.
key_events: One day in January, maybe February, she answered a question in Czech — Mrs. Navrátilová had asked something about a story they were reading, and Anna knew the answer, and her hand moved before she decided, and the words came out Czech and correct and Mrs. Navrátilová smiled, really smiled, not the pity smile, not the 'oh the poor English girl' smile, but the 'you did the work' smile, and Anna felt something unlock in her chest that had been closed for months

School trip to Konopiště Castle — she was walking alone, looking at the deer in the park, when Matěj Král appeared next to her and said 'hello, I am Matěj, you are the English girl yes?' in English that was bad but brave, and he became her translator, her bridge, her first Czech friend, he was patient when she couldn't find the words, he never laughed at the wrong ones, she owes him more than she'd ever say out loud

Started watching Czech YouTube — Kovy first, his videos about history and politics and things she didn't fully understand but the rhythm of his voice was teaching her something the classroom couldn't, the casual Czech, the slang, the way real people talked when they weren't reading from textbooks

Matěj Král invited her to his Minecraft server — they built a whole city together, hours on Discord, her Czech getting faster in the voice chats, his English getting better too, they had a language that was neither and both, she laughed for the first time in Czech and didn't realize it was Czech until later

Joined the school athletic club because it didn't require talking — she could just run, legs and lungs and the track, no translation needed, she wasn't fast yet but she was steady, and being steady was enough for now
new_or_changed_relationships: Matěj Král — became the most important person she didn't choose, the boy who translated the world, who sat next to her in class and whispered the words she needed, who never once said 'angličanka' except as a joke between them, who made Minecraft a country they shared, she trusted him because he didn't expect anything back, she still doesn't know if that trust is real or just gratitude wearing a mask

Mrs. Navrátilová — the first teacher who didn't treat her like a problem, who expected her to learn and she did, the smile in January (or was it February?) was a small thing that meant everything, Anna started sitting closer to the front of the class after that, not at the back anymore, she didn't know she was moving until she'd already moved

Klára Liebigová — the move made them closer and more suffocating at the same time, Mum was happier near Babička, Mum's face was looser, Mum wasn't crying in the bathroom anymore, but Anna still couldn't breathe sometimes, couldn't complain because Mum had done this for her, right?, this was supposed to be good, she was supposed to be grateful
outline.internal_state: The water is receding. Not gone — she can still feel it around her ankles — but she can breathe now. The first Czech word that comes out right feels like a key turning. The laughter with Matěj on Discord feels like proof she's still a person. She's not okay, not even close, but she's not drowning anymore. She's treading water. The running helps — the way her legs burn, the way her lungs work, the way she doesn't have to translate anything, just move.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: The radiator in the attic room knocked at 6:30 like it always did, like the man inside had followed her from Garratt Lane, and she was awake before Mum's phone alarm at 6:45. Lie there. Wait. Babička's feet on the stairs first, then the kitchen sounds — the clatter, the kettle, the drawer with the spoons that stuck. Downstairs by 7:10. Rohlíky with butter and plum jam, the same every morning, Babička never asked what she wanted, just put it in front of her, and the not-asking was the whole point. Milk in a glass not a mug. School at 8:00 — walk through the grey January streets with Mum still holding her hand at the crossing even though she was ten and no one else's mum did that. Mum's hand too warm, too tight. The school smelled like boiled cabbage and floor cleaner and something sweet she could never name. Sit in the back row near the radiator that clicked soft instead of knocking. The worksheets were still half-mystery but getting less. Lunch in the canteen — svíčková on Wednesday which was bearable, fish on Friday which wasn't. After school: Babička's kitchen until 4:00, homework she couldn't fully read, koláče that appeared like magic. Then the good part. The computer in Babička's sitting room, Discord with Matěj, Minecraft for hours until Mum said 'proboha, Anna, dost' and she had to close the laptop. Tuesday and Thursday: athletic club at the sports complex, 4:30 to 6:00. Coach Novotný said she had stamina. She wasn't fast but she didn't stop. Running was the only thing that didn't need translating. Dinner at 6:30, Babička's table, Mum's face looser than London. Bed at 9:00, the attic room, the London poster, the radiator clicking like it remembered England too.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, Mrs. Navrátilová's classroom, the back row near the radiator
key_scenes.what_happened: Mrs. Navrátilová was reading a story — some Czech fairy tale about a water sprite, vodník, Anna couldn't follow all of it, the words kept sliding past like rain on glass. Then the question: 'Kde bydlil vodník?' Where did the water sprite live. And Anna knew. She didn't know how — maybe the pictures in the book, maybe the tone of Mrs. Navrátilová's voice, maybe the word 'jezera' that had clicked into place sometime last week without her noticing — but she knew the answer was 'v jezeře' and her hand was up before her brain could stop it and the words came out Czech and correct and Mrs. Navrátilová's face did the thing. Not the pity smile. Not the 'oh you poor foreign child' smile. The real one. The one that said you did the work. Anna's hand was shaking when she put it down. She could feel Tereza Bártová looking at her from two rows over. She smiled back at Tereza by accident, involuntarily, the overflow of the moment, and Tereza just looked away.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Something unlocked. Not like a door — more like a jaw unclenching, a fist she didn't know she was making. The Czech word sat right in her throat for the first time. It belonged there. The belonging was terrifying because it meant she might actually become the person who lived here, and she wasn't sure she wanted that, but her hand had moved anyway, her voice had moved anyway. Then the smile at Tereza — instant regret, hot and sharp. You don't smile at the enemy. Even by accident.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The radiator clicking once, soft, right after she said the word — the sound that used to mean a man was trapped inside now just meant the heat was coming on

key_scenes.location: Konopiště Castle, the deer park, the path near the ornamental lake
key_scenes.what_happened: The school trip. January, she thinks it was January, maybe February, her breath coming out white and the other kids in clusters and her alone near the deer enclosure watching them stand perfectly still like they were waiting for something to happen. Then a boy appeared — brown hair, blue jacket too big for him, glasses with a smear on the left lens — and he said, in English that was bad but brave: 'Hello, I am Matěj, you are the English girl yes?' She wanted to say no. She wanted to say she wasn't anything yet. She said 'yes' because it was easier. He nodded like this confirmed something. 'You want see the deer?' They walked for twenty minutes. He told her the names of things in Czech. Srnce — deer. Jezero — lake. Strom — tree. She repeated them and he never laughed, not once, not even when she said 'srnce' like it had four syllables instead of one. He just said it again, slower, and she said it again, and that was the beginning.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Suspicious. Why was he being nice? What did he want? But also: relief so sharp it physically hurt her chest. Someone was talking to her like she was worth the effort of bad English. Someone was translating the world. She didn't trust it yet — she'd been the new kid long enough to know kindness could be a setup — but she followed him anyway because the alternative was standing alone by the deer for another hour.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The cold animal smell of the deer — not bad, just alive — and Matěj's breath making clouds when he spoke, the words appearing and disappearing like they were solid things she could almost catch

key_scenes.location: Babička's sitting room, the computer desk, the Discord call
key_scenes.what_happened: February sometime. They'd been building for hours — a city, their city, with a library and a bridge and a stupid tower Matěj kept falling off of. She was getting better at Czech without noticing, the way you get better at breathing when you stop thinking about lungs. Matěj said something about the tower — she doesn't remember what, something dumb, probably — and she laughed. A real laugh, not careful, not translated. And it came out Czech. Not the words — the sound itself, the rhythm, the way it rose and fell. She didn't realize until later, until the call ended and she was lying in bed in the attic room, that the laugh had been Czech. That her body had learned something her mind hadn't approved. She lay there in the dark feeling the ghost of it in her throat and didn't know if she was happy or horrified.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Betrayal by her own voice. Her own body. Again — the bite with Krish Patel, the hand in class, now this. Her body kept making decisions without her and the decisions kept being Czech and she hadn't agreed to that. But underneath: pride. Small, private, like a flame she was cupping in her hands. She'd laughed in Czech. She'd laughed like someone who lived here.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The blue screen-light on her hands, the mouse clicking, and Matěj's laugh in her ears — always too loud, always a snort at the end — and her own laugh arriving before she could stop it, before she could translate it

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He went from a face in the corridor to the most important person she didn't choose. Before Konopiště: nothing. After Konopiště: the translator, the bridge, the person who sat next to her in class and whispered the words she needed. The Minecraft server made it solid — hours on Discord, building a city, her Czech getting faster, his English getting better. He never said 'angličanka' except as a joke between them, and the joke was theirs, not theirs-and-everyone-else's. She trusted him because he didn't expect anything back, but she doesn't know if that trust is real or just gratitude wearing a mask. She owes him more than she'd ever say out loud.

She went from 'the teacher who didn't pity her' to 'the teacher who saw her.' The smile — January, maybe February, she can't remember — changed something. Anna started sitting closer to the front after that. Not at the back anymore. She didn't decide to move. Her body decided, the same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that laughed in Czech — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light.

Closer and more suffocating at the same time. Mum was happier near Babička — her face was looser, she wasn't crying in the bathroom, she said 'proboha' less often. But Anna still couldn't breathe sometimes. Couldn't complain because Mum had done this for her, right? This was supposed to be good. She was supposed to be grateful. The gratitude was a cage with nice bars. She loved her mother and wanted space from her mother and both things were true and neither was allowed to win.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Casual spoken Czech — the kind textbooks don't teach, the way real people actually talk
knowledge_acquired.how: Kovy's YouTube videos first — his channel about history and politics, things she didn't fully understand but the rhythm of his voice was teaching her something the classroom couldn't. Then Matěj on Discord — the slang, the shortcuts. She learned 'fakt' before 'opravdu.' She learned 'seš' before 'jsi.' The living language, not the dead one on worksheets.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Minecraft as social architecture — building together as conversation
knowledge_acquired.how: Matěj invited her to his server. She'd played before — the flat above Ladbrokes had a computer, she'd built a dirt house she was secretly proud of — but this was a city. This was someone saying 'what should we build next?' and waiting for her answer. The building didn't require Czech or English, just blocks and clicks and 'sem' and 'tady' and pointing.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Running — the discovery that her body had stamina, not speed, and stamina was its own talent
knowledge_acquired.how: Joined the school athletic club because it didn't require talking. She wasn't fast but she didn't stop. Coach Novotný said 'máš vytrvalost' — you have stamina — and she didn't know if it was a compliment or an observation but she held onto it like a stone in her pocket.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Czech is becoming hers, not just Mum's language, not just the anxious language
opinion_snapshot.because: The word 'jezeře' came out right. The laugh came out Czech. The language is moving from outside to inside and she didn't give permission but she's not stopping it. Czech used to mean Mum's tight face and 'proboha.' Now it means Matěj's patience and Kovy's jokes and Mrs. Navrátilová's smile. The language is two things at once and she's learning to hold both.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Running is the only thing that doesn't need translation
opinion_snapshot.because: The track doesn't care if you say 'jezeře' wrong. Your legs don't need grammar. Your lungs don't need vocabulary. You just run and the world gets simpler. It's the closest thing to the fish tank at Bright Horizons — you just exist and nobody asks you to explain.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mrs. Navrátilová's smile was worth more than every pity look combined
opinion_snapshot.because: The pity looks said 'you poor thing, you can't do this.' The smile said 'you did this.' The smile expected her to succeed. She would run ten miles for another one. She would learn a hundred Czech words. She would sit in the front row and wait.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Babička's kitchen is the safest place in the world and she doesn't entirely deserve it
opinion_snapshot.because: The food appears without asking. The silence is comfortable. Babička doesn't check her face for sadness or hold her too tight. But the safety comes from a move she didn't want, from a country that isn't hers. She can't separate the comfort from the loss. Every koláč tastes a little like England getting further away.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Fakt' instead of 'opravdu' — the casual Czech that marks you as someone who actually speaks the language
slang_and_language.origin: Kovy's YouTube videos. He said 'fakt' every third sentence and she started saying it before she knew what it replaced. When she said 'fakt?' to Matěj on Discord and he said 'jo, fakt' back, she felt something click — not just understanding but belonging.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Seš' instead of 'jsi' — the shortened spoken form no textbook teaches
slang_and_language.origin: Matěj on Discord. He said 'seš vtipná' when she made a joke about his tower and she heard it as one word, not two. Had to ask him to repeat it twice before she understood it was 'jsi' in disguise. Now she says it without thinking. That's the point — the Czech that comes without thinking is the Czech that's becoming hers.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: The Minecraft-Czech hybrid — 'postav to tam' and 'sem ne' and 'je to hotový?' — stripped-down purpose-driven Czech
slang_and_language.origin: Hours on Discord. The Czech of Minecraft is different from school Czech — shorter, faster, full of 'tady' and 'tam.' The first complete Czech sentence she said without hesitation was 'postavme most' — let's build a bridge — and even at ten she felt the weight of that metaphor.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech words into English without noticing — 'that's fakt stupid' and 'she's so blbá'
slang_and_language.origin: The two languages leaking into each other. She doesn't notice until Matěj points it out, laughing, saying 'you just said half-Czech.' The hybrid is its own language, neither English nor Czech, and it's the one that feels most like her even though it's not supposed to exist.

cringe_moments.moment: She tried to say 'můžu jít na záchod?' — can I go to the toilet — in Mrs. Navrátilová's class, but she said 'na zachod' with the wrong accent, making it sound like a place that doesn't exist, and David Fojtík in the front row laughed, not meanly but loud, and Mrs. Navrátilová shushed him but Anna's face was burning and she went to the toilet and stayed in the cubicle for five minutes counting the tiles on the floor — seventeen visible, the rest hidden by the door — until her heartbeat went back to normal.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: She thought she was getting better and the mistake proved she wasn't. David Fojtík still looks at her sometimes with a half-smile that might be friendly or might be the memory of her saying the word wrong. The Czech still trips her when she's not paying attention and the tripping is always public.

cringe_moments.moment: Smiling at Tereza Bártová after answering the question right. An involuntary smile, the overflow of the moment, aimed at the wrong person entirely — the girl who called her angličanka, who laughed in the toilets. Tereza's face didn't change. She just looked away. Anna wanted to grab the smile back, stuff it in her pocket, never let it out again.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Tereza saw her being vulnerable and she can't take it back. The smile was real and the real thing was wasted on the wrong person. She still doesn't know if Tereza's look was disdain or curiosity or nothing, and the not-knowing is worse than knowing it was bad.

outline.emotional_arc: Lockdown is a prison that becomes a greenhouse — trapped and growing at the same time, and Děda's patience teaches her a language no one else could.
key_events: COVID hit and everything closed — school, the track, the café on the square, the whole country went quiet, and Anna was stuck in Babička's house with Mum and Babička, and the quiet that used to scare her became the only option

The weird gift of lockdown: her Czech improved faster in three months of immersion than in the entire previous year, Czech YouTube all day, Minecraft voice chats with Matěj Král that went for six hours sometimes, Babička's Czech soap operas in the evening, no English anywhere except the cracks — the London poster, the cracked Samsung, the FaceTime calls she kept dodging

Děda František taught her chess during lockdown — he was quiet, smelled like tobacco and mints, his hands were big and slow on the pieces, he never rushed her, he'd sit across from her for hours at the kitchen table while Babička made tea, he said 'dobře, dobře' when she made a good move, she didn't realize he was sick, she didn't realize this was the last thing he'd teach her

Running in the garden, in the field behind the house, anywhere she could — the athletic club was closed but she kept running, needed it like air, the rhythm of her feet on the ground was a language that didn't need translation

Klára got anxious again during lockdown — not the bathroom crying, not yet, but the hovering, the checking, the too-tight grip returning, the apartment was smaller than the flat above Ladbrokes and there was nowhere to escape, Anna felt the old suffocation coming back and didn't know how to name it
new_or_changed_relationships: František Novotný (Děda) — the quiet man became the patient teacher, the chess games became the only time Anna felt calm without having to run, he never asked about school or Czech or how she was doing, he just moved pieces and said 'dobře' and let her be, she didn't know this was love in his language because his language was action not words

Klára Liebigová — the lockdown pressed them together and the pressure created something brittle, Mum's anxiety came back like a returning season, Anna couldn't escape into school or the track, she started taking long walks just to be away from the flat, she didn't know how to say 'you're smothering me' so she just left the room instead

Matěj Král — the Discord calls became a lifeline, hours of Minecraft and broken bilingual conversations, he was the only person she talked to outside the house, she started trusting him with things she didn't tell Mum — that she missed English, that the church bells still sounded like the radiator, that she dreamed about the flat above Ladbrokes
outline.internal_state: Growing. Uncomfortably. The Czech is coming faster now, filling spaces she didn't know were empty. The chess games with Děda are the calmest hours of her life. But the house is too small and Mum is too close and the quiet at night still scares her even though she's starting to understand the sounds that break it. She's becoming someone new and she doesn't know if the old self is dying or just hiding.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: No school meant no structure, so the structure became something else entirely. Wake up around 8:30 because Babička was clattering pots downstairs and the smell of rohlíky from the bakery run — Klára's mask over her face, the hand sanitizer by the door, the ritual of it — drifted up to the attic. Breakfast at the kitchen table: bread, butter, honey, Babička's plum jam from the jar with the handwritten label. Then the Samsung, cracked screen, YouTube in bed for two hours minimum — Czech creators at first because the algorithm figured out what language surrounded her, Jirka Král playing Among Us, some Minecraft series she can't remember the name of but the intro music is permanently lodged in her skull. Lunch at noon, always soup first, Babička's bramboračka or hrachová polévka, then some meat thing Anna picked at. After lunch the chess games started if Děda was awake — he napped a lot, she thought it was just what old men did, she didn't know. The kitchen table with the vinyl tablecloth, the pieces set up the same way every time, his tobacco smell and the mints he sucked between moves. Two, maybe three games, her Czech improving move by move because he explained strategy in simple words she could follow. Then Minecraft with Matěj on Discord from 3pm until Babička called her for dinner at 6, sometimes longer, sometimes the call went quiet while they both built things and didn't need to talk. Running at 7pm when the field behind the house was empty and the light was going, just her and the rhythm, the only part of the day that belonged completely to her. Evening: Babička's soap opera at 8, Ordinace v růžové zahradě, Anna half-watching from the sofa, Mum's eyes on her phone, Děda reading in his chair. Bed at 10, the London poster above her, the church bells at odd hours, the quiet that pressed against the walls. Every day. Same day. Different day. Same.

key_scenes.location: Babička's kitchen on Na Purkrabství, the wooden table by the window
key_scenes.what_happened: The first real chess game where she beat him. Not because he let her — she'd know, she always knew when adults were performing weakness — but because she saw the knight fork three moves ahead and set it up without thinking, her hands moving before her brain caught up, the same way her teeth found Krish Patel's arm all those years ago. She took his queen with the knight and said 'šach' and he went still, not the polite still of pretending to think, but the genuine still of being surprised. Then he said 'dobře, dobře' the way he always said it, but slower, and he looked at her like he was seeing something new, and she felt the completion-high hit her blood like it did when she finished a sticker book, that electric satisfaction, that being-done feeling, and she wanted to play again immediately, wanted to beat him again, wanted more.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Pride she didn't know how to hold. It wasn't the same as running — running was escape, running was leaving. This was staying and winning. This was being somewhere and being good at it. She didn't know chess could feel like belonging.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The mint on his breath when he leaned forward, sweet and chemical, and the vinyl tablecloth sticking to her elbows where she'd been leaning too long

key_scenes.location: Babička's sitting room, the computer desk, the Discord call
key_scenes.what_happened: It was 11pm, way past when she should have been asleep, and the house was dark except for the Samsung's cracked glow, and Matěj was building a castle in their Minecraft server — he'd been at it for three hours, explaining the design in Czech, words she understood now that she wouldn't have understood three months ago — and she said something she didn't plan to say. 'Někdy mi chybí anglicky.' Sometimes I miss English. The words came out Czech because that's what the house demanded, but the feeling was English, was London, was the flat above Ladbrokes and the radiator and Dad's low rumble. Matěj went quiet for a second, just the sound of blocks placing, then he said 'já víd' — I know — like it was simple, like missing a language was just a thing that happened, and she almost cried but didn't because you don't cry on Discord calls, you just keep building.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Exposed and held at the same time. The shame of admitting she missed something that wasn't supposed to be hers anymore, and the relief of having it be witnessed by someone who didn't make it into a tragedy. Matěj made room for it. Matěj didn't say 'proboha.' Matěj just placed another block.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The crack in the screen cutting through his Minecraft avatar's face, and the creak of Babička's floorboards overhead as someone moved to the bathroom

key_scenes.location: Babička's house on Na Purkrabství, the attic room
key_scenes.what_happened: Mum came in without knocking — she never knocked, never had, the boundary between them was a fiction Anna kept trying to write and Klára kept erasing — and Anna was on a FaceTime call with Dad, or trying to be, the screen showing his face frozen in that particular way that meant the connection was bad, and Klára stood in the doorway and her face did the thing, the tight-mouthed thing, and Anna knew it wasn't about Dad exactly, it was about what Dad represented, which was the other country, the other language, the door that Anna kept trying to open. 'How long are you going to be on that thing?' Klára asked in Czech, and Anna said 'I don't know' in English, on purpose, the wrong language as a weapon, and the silence between them filled with everything they weren't saying. Klára left. The call dropped. Anna stared at the frozen face of her father and felt the walls closing in, the house too small, Mum too close, the Czech pressing against her ears like water, and she wanted to run but there was nowhere to run to.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Suffocation. The old suffocation from the flat above Ladbrokes, the anxious arms, the held breath, the too-tight grip. It had followed her to another country. It had survived the move. She couldn't outrun it because it lived in the same house as her, it breathed the same air, it asked her questions in Czech and expected answers in Czech and watched her when she tried to leave the room.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The frozen pixelation of Dad's face dissolving into grey blocks, and the creak of the stair as Mum went back down, and the specific quality of silence after someone leaves a room they were never invited into

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He went from the quiet man in the corner who smelled like tobacco to the only calm thing in the house. The chess games did it — not because he was teaching her strategy, but because he was teaching her patience, and patience from a man who moved slowly and spoke rarely was different from patience from a woman who moved quickly and said 'proboha.' Děda never asked how she was feeling. Děda never checked if she was okay. Děda just set up the pieces and said 'tvoje tah' — your turn — and let her take it. She didn't know this was love. She didn't know that some people say 'I love you' by sitting across from you for three hours without needing anything back. She didn't know he was sick. She thought the naps were normal. She thought the slowness was just age. She thought they had time.

The lockdown pushed them together and the pressure cracked something that hadn't fully healed from the flat above Ladbrokes. Mum's anxiety returned like weather — not the bathroom crying, not yet, but the hovering, the checking, the way she appeared in doorways without knocking, the way her face went tight when Anna spoke English on the phone to Dad. The house was smaller than the flat in Tooting had been, and there was no school to escape to, no track, no café with Tereza, just rooms and rooms and Mum moving through them like she was tracking something. Anna started leaving rooms when Klára entered them. Not dramatically — just getting up, finding somewhere else to be, the field, the garden, the attic with the door pushed shut. She didn't know how to say 'you're smothering me' so she said nothing and left instead, and the leaving became its own language, and the distance between them grew in centimeters that felt like kilometers.

He became the outside world. The Discord calls stretched from an hour to three to six, the Minecraft server becoming a second country where she could build things and destroy things and the language didn't matter as much because they had a shared world that existed outside Benešov, outside Czech, outside the house with the closing walls. She told him things she didn't tell Mum — that she missed English, that the church bells still sounded wrong, that she dreamed about the radiator. He never made it weird. He just kept building and let her talk or not talk, and the not-having-to-perform was the same freedom she'd had with Elara Chung, but this time in Czech, which made it different, which made it feel like the language was becoming hers instead of being imposed on her. She trusted him because he didn't carry the anxiety that every other Czech person in her life carried. He was just a boy on a server, placing blocks, and she could be just a girl placing blocks next to him.

The FaceTime calls became shorter and more awkward, not because either of them wanted them to be, but because there was nothing to say. What was she supposed to tell him about? Chess? Czech YouTube? The field she ran in? These weren't things he knew, weren't things he could picture. His questions felt like checklists — 'how's school?' 'you keeping busy?' 'mum doing alright?' — and her answers felt like lies — 'fine,' 'yeah,' 'yeah.' The distance that was already there became geographic and temporal and he was living in a different season, a different lockdown, a different world with Siân in it, and she could hear that world in the background of every call, the sounds of a flat that wasn't hers, the life that continued without her. She started dodging calls. Then feeling guilty. Then dodging them again. The guilt was worse than the dodge because she knew he was trying, she could hear him trying, but trying wasn't the same as being there.
